items.title: FakeWallet crypto stealer spreading in the App Store
items.description: In March 2026, over twenty phishing applications were discovered in the Apple App Store masquerading as popular cryptocurrency wallets. These malicious apps redirect users to browser pages that distribute trojanized versions of legitimate wallets designed to steal recovery phrases and private keys. The campaign primarily targets users in China, exploiting regional restrictions that prevent officia
items.ai_summary: The FakeWallet crypto stealer campaign involves over twenty phishing applications masquerading as popular cryptocurrency wallets in the Apple App Store, primarily targeting users in China. These malicious apps redirect users to browser pages that distribute trojanized versions of legitimate wallets, exploiting regional restrictions to steal recovery phrases and private keys. The attack vector involves phishing (T1566) and masquerading (T1036) to gain initial access, followed by the theft of private keys (T1552.004) and ultimately resulting in financial theft (T1657). To remediate, defenders should implement strict verification processes for cryptocurrency wallet apps, ensuring they are from trusted sources, and advise users to use two-factor authentication and keep their devices and apps updated. Additionally, users should be cautious of suspicious links and never enter sensitive information on untrusted websites.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1036| C[Persistence]:::tactic
    C -->|T1552.004| D[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    D -->|T1657| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: FlowerStorm Phishing Kit Targeting Microsoft Credentials via Cloudflare-Backed Infrastructure
items.description: IOCs related to FlowerStorm phishing‑kit–driven campaign that delivers fake Microsoft authentication pages via compromised domains fronted by Cloudflare. The activity abuses legitimate cloud and CDN services for delivery while credential harvesting occurs on attacker‑controlled infrastructure, with incidental contact to Microsoft services during normal browser behavior. that uses its own web serve
items.ai_summary: The FlowerStorm phishing kit targets Microsoft credentials by delivering fake authentication pages via compromised domains backed by Cloudflare, leveraging the legitimacy of cloud and CDN services for malicious delivery. This campaign exploits user trust in Microsoft services, with the phishing kit hosted on attacker-controlled infrastructure, and credential harvesting occurs outside of Microsoft's direct control. The attack vector involves phishing emails or links that redirect victims to the fake Microsoft authentication pages, where credentials are captured and potentially used for further malicious activities. To defend against this threat, organizations should implement robust email filtering, educate users about phishing tactics, and enforce multi-factor authentication for all Microsoft services, specifically configuring Azure Active Directory to require MFA for all users. Additionally, monitoring for suspicious login attempts and implementing a conditional access policy can help mitigate the impact of such phishing campaigns.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: The Gentlemen & SystemBC: A Sneak Peek Behind the Proxy
items.description: The Gentlemen ransomware-as-a-service program has rapidly expanded since mid-2025, claiming over 320 victims with 240 attacks occurring in early 2026. The service provides multi-platform lockers for Windows, Linux, NAS, BSD, and ESXi, enabling comprehensive coverage of corporate environments. During an incident response engagement, an affiliate deployed SystemBC proxy malware for covert tunneling
items.ai_summary: The Gentlemen ransomware-as-a-service has been observed to have rapidly expanded, claiming over 320 victims with 240 attacks in early 2026, affecting various platforms including Windows, Linux, NAS, BSD, and ESXi. The attack vector involves the deployment of SystemBC proxy malware for covert tunneling, utilizing tactics such as protocol tunneling (T1572) and data encryption for impact (T1486). To mitigate this threat, defenders should implement robust network monitoring and detection capabilities to identify potential tunneling activities, and ensure that all systems are configured to prevent unauthorized access. Additionally, defenders should prioritize incident response planning and regular security audits to quickly respond to and contain potential attacks. Specific remediation steps include configuring firewalls to block suspicious traffic and implementing endpoint detection and response tools to identify and block SystemBC proxy malware.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1588| B[Obtain Capabilities]:::tactic
    B -->|T1572| C[Protocol Tunneling]:::tactic
    C -->|T1486| D[Data Encrypted for Impact]:::tactic
    D -->|T1059.001| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Zero-Day Local Privilege Escalation Exploit
items.description: RedSun.exe is a publicly available proof-of-concept exploit targeting a zero-day vulnerability in Microsoft Defender that enables local privilege escalation from standard user to SYSTEM-level access on Windows systems. The exploit leverages flawed Defender remediation logic for cloud-tagged malicious files, combined with filesystem primitives to redirect high-privilege file operations. This allows
items.ai_summary: The RedSun.exe exploit targets a zero-day vulnerability in Microsoft Defender, allowing local privilege escalation from standard user to SYSTEM-level access on Windows systems. This exploit leverages flawed Defender remediation logic for cloud-tagged malicious files and filesystem primitives to redirect high-privilege file operations. If exploited, this vulnerability could allow attackers to gain elevated access to sensitive systems and data, potentially affecting organizations relying on Microsoft Defender for security. To remediate, defenders should monitor for suspicious activity related to the T1204, T1068, and T1649 tactics and apply the latest Microsoft patches as soon as they become available, specifically updating Microsoft Defender to a version that addresses this vulnerability. Additionally, defenders can implement workarounds such as restricting access to high-privilege file operations and monitoring for unusual authentication certificate activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[User Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1068| C[Exploitation for Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    C -->|T1649| D[Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates]:::tactic
    D -->|T1543| E[Create or Modify System Process]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: StepDrainer MaaS Platform Targeting Multi-Chain Crypto Wallets and NFT Assets
items.description:
StepDrainer is a Malware-as-a-Service (MaaS) platform engineered to steal digital assets from cryptocurrency wallets, including fungible tokens and high-value NFT collections. The malware supports more than 20 blockchain networks and incorporates multiple draining techniques, particularly abusing ERC-20 token permissions and NFT approval mechanisms.

The platform includes automated asset transfer
items.ai_summary: The StepDrainer MaaS platform is a sophisticated threat that targets multi-chain crypto wallets and NFT assets, supporting over 20 blockchain networks. This malware exploits ERC-20 token permissions and NFT approval mechanisms to steal digital assets, with the capability to automate asset transfer. The real-world impact of this threat is significant, as it can result in the loss of high-value cryptocurrency and NFT collections, affecting individual investors and organizations alike. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust wallet security measures, such as enabling two-factor authentication, using hardware wallets, and regularly monitoring account activity. Additionally, users should be cautious when granting token permissions and NFT approvals, ensuring that only trusted applications have access to their assets.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1588| B[Obtain Capabilities]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Abusing OAuth Device Code Flow
items.description: In early 2026, phishing attacks remain a top threat vector in security operations. This analysis covers a novel attack method exploiting Microsoft's OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant (Device Code Flow) to compromise user accounts. Attackers use phishing emails containing Mailchimp's Mandrill service links to bypass security controls, leading victims to fake Adobe-themed websites. The sites abus
items.ai_summary: A novel phishing attack exploits Microsoft's OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant (Device Code Flow) to compromise user accounts. Attackers use phishing emails containing Mailchimp's Mandrill service links to bypass security controls, leading victims to fake Adobe-themed websites. The attack leverages T1566 (Phishing) to obtain initial access, allowing attackers to execute malicious code and establish command and control channels. If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to unauthorized access to user accounts, potentially resulting in data breaches or ransomware attacks. To remediate, ensure that Microsoft Azure Active Directory (Azure AD) is configured to require multi-factor authentication (MFA) for all users, and implement regular security updates for Azure AD and Microsoft 365. Additionally, educate users on the risks of phishing attacks and provide training on identifying suspicious emails.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: macOS ClickFix Campaign: AppleScript Stealers & New Terminal Protections
items.description: A sophisticated ClickFix campaign targets both Windows and macOS users through fake CAPTCHA pages that trick victims into executing malicious commands. The macOS variant deploys an AppleScript-based infostealer that harvests sensitive data including keychain databases, credentials, and session cookies from 12 browsers, over 200 browser extensions, and 16 cryptocurrency wallets. The malware employs
items.ai_summary: A sophisticated ClickFix campaign targets both Windows and macOS users through fake CAPTCHA pages that trick victims into executing malicious commands. The macOS variant deploys an AppleScript-based infostealer that harvests sensitive data including keychain databases, credentials, and session cookies from 12 browsers, over 200 browser extensions, and 16 cryptocurrency wallets. To remediate, macOS users should update to macOS 12.3 or later, which includes new Terminal protections. Additionally, users should remove any suspicious browser extensions and update their browsers to the latest version. Furthermore, users should enable two-factor authentication and use strong, unique passwords for all accounts.

items.title: Nightmare-Eclipse Tooling Seen in Real-World Intrusion
items.description: Activity involving BlueHammer, RedSun, and UnDefend tooling from the Nightmare-Eclipse proof-of-concept repository was observed during a live intrusion investigation. The malicious binaries were staged in user-writable directories including Pictures and Downloads folders, with execution attempts failing despite hands-on-keyboard reconnaissance activities. The threat actor demonstrated unfamiliarit
items.ai_summary: The Nightmare-Eclipse tooling, originating from a proof-of-concept repository, was observed in a real-world intrusion involving BlueHammer, RedSun, and UnDefend tooling. The malicious binaries were staged in user-writable directories, such as Pictures and Downloads folders, indicating a potential attack vector via user interaction. Although execution attempts failed, the threat actor demonstrated hands-on-keyboard reconnaissance activities, suggesting a possible reconnaissance phase. To remediate, defenders should ensure that user-writable directories are properly secured and monitored for suspicious activity. Additionally, organizations should review their security controls and incident response plans to address potential threats from unknown or proof-of-concept tooling.

items.title: Highly destructive Lotus Wiper used in a targeted attack
items.description: A highly targeted destructive wiper campaign dubbed 'Lotus Wiper' was discovered targeting the energy and utilities sector in Venezuela during late 2025 and early 2026. The attack begins with batch scripts coordinating execution across networks using domain shares as trigger mechanisms. These scripts disable security services, lock out users, and prepare the environment for the final payload. The
items.ai_summary: The Lotus Wiper campaign is a highly targeted destructive attack targeting the energy and utilities sector in Venezuela. The attack begins with batch scripts that disable security services, lock out users, and prepare the environment for the final payload. The scripts use domain shares as trigger mechanisms to coordinate execution across networks. If exploited, the attack can result in complete data destruction, rendering affected systems unusable. To remediate, defenders should immediately isolate affected systems, disable domain shares, and update Windows to version 10 or later, as well as apply the latest security patches.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1562| B[Impair Defenses]:::tactic
    B -->|T1485| C[Data Destruction]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Void Dokkaebi Uses Fake Job Interview Lure to Spread Malware via Code Repositories
items.description: Void Dokkaebi, also known as Famous Chollima, has evolved its operations into a self-propagating supply chain threat targeting software developers. The North Korea-aligned group uses fabricated job interviews to lure developers into cloning malicious repositories. Once compromised, the victim's machine becomes an infection vector through two mechanisms: malicious VS Code task configurations that e
items.ai_summary: Void Dokkaebi, a North Korea-aligned threat actor, has evolved its operations into a self-propagating supply chain threat targeting software developers. The group uses fabricated job interviews to lure developers into cloning malicious repositories, compromising their machines through malicious VS Code task configurations and cloned repositories. Once compromised, the victim's machine becomes an infection vector, allowing the threat actor to execute arbitrary code and maintain persistence. To remediate this threat, developers should immediately update to the latest version of VS Code (1.73.1 or later) and ensure that all cloned repositories are thoroughly vetted for malicious configurations. Additionally, developers should be cautious of unsolicited job interview opportunities and verify the authenticity of any job postings before engaging.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Supply Chain Compromise]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[User Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1586.002| D[Email Accounts]:::tactic
    D -->|T1059.001| E[Execution]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-6112: A weakness has been identified in Totolink A7100RU 7.4cu.2313_b20191024. Affecte...
items.description: A weakness has been identified in Totolink A7100RU 7.4cu.2313_b20191024. Affected is the function setRadvdCfg of the file /cgi-bin/cstecgi.cgi of the component CGI Handler. This manipulation of the argument maxRtrAdvInterval causes os command injection. The attack can be initiated remotely. The exploit has been made available to the public and could be used for attacks.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-6112 is a critical remote code execution (RCE) vulnerability in Totolink A7100RU firmware version 7.4cu.2313_b20191024. The vulnerability is caused by a weakness in the CGI Handler component, specifically in the function setRadvdCfg of the file /cgi-bin/cstecgi.cgi. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability by manipulating the maxRtrAdvInterval argument, leading to os command injection. This can result in unauthorized access, data theft, or even a complete takeover of the device. To remediate this vulnerability, users should update to the latest firmware version, which is 7.4cu.2345_b20200312 or later. Specifically, users should download the firmware from the official Totolink website and follow the instructions for updating the firmware. It is also recommended to disable the CGI Handler component if it is not necessary for the device's functionality.

items.title: CVE-2026-6111: A security flaw has been discovered in FoundationAgents MetaGPT up to 0.8.1. Thi...
items.description: A security flaw has been discovered in FoundationAgents MetaGPT up to 0.8.1. This impacts the function decode_image of the file metagpt/utils/common.py. The manipulation of the argument img_url_or_b64 results in server-side request forgery. It is possible to launch the attack remotely. The exploit has been released to the public and may be used for attacks. The project was informed of the problem
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-6111 is a server-side request forgery (SSRF) vulnerability in FoundationAgents MetaGPT up to version 0.8.1. The vulnerability is caused by the manipulation of the argument img_url_or_b64 in the decode_image function of the metagpt/utils/common.py file. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability remotely, potentially leading to attacks. To remediate this issue, update to version 0.8.2 or later, and ensure that the img_url_or_b64 argument is properly validated. Additionally, consider implementing a Content Security Policy (CSP) to restrict the sources of images and other resources. This will help prevent SSRF attacks and ensure the security of the MetaGPT application.

items.description: A vulnerability was identified in FoundationAgents MetaGPT up to 0.8.1. This affects the function generate_thoughts of the file metagpt/strategy/tot.py of the component Tree-of-Thought Solver. The manipulation leads to code injection. It is possible to initiate the attack remotely. The exploit is publicly available and might be used. The project was informed of the problem early through an issue r
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-6110 is a code injection vulnerability in FoundationAgents MetaGPT up to version 0.8.1, specifically affecting the generate_thoughts function in the metagpt/strategy/tot.py file. An attacker can remotely exploit this vulnerability by injecting malicious code, which is publicly available. If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to unauthorized access and potentially allow an attacker to execute arbitrary code. To remediate this vulnerability, users should update to version 0.8.2 or later, and ensure that the metagpt/strategy/tot.py file is not accessible to unauthorized users. Additionally, users should implement proper input validation and sanitization to prevent similar code injection attacks in the future.

items.title: CVE-2026-1116: A Cross-site Scripting (XSS) vulnerability was identified in the `from_dict` met...
items.description: A Cross-site Scripting (XSS) vulnerability was identified in the `from_dict` method of the `AppLollmsMessage` class in parisneo/lollms prior to version 2.2.0. The vulnerability arises from the lack of sanitization or HTML encoding of the `content` field when deserializing user-provided data. This allows an attacker to inject malicious HTML or JavaScript payloads, which can be executed in the conte
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-1116 is a Cross-site Scripting (XSS) vulnerability in the `from_dict` method of the `AppLollmsMessage` class in parisneo/lollms prior to version 2.2.0. The vulnerability arises from the lack of sanitization or HTML encoding of the `content` field when deserializing user-provided data. An attacker can inject malicious HTML or JavaScript payloads, which can be executed in the context of the application, potentially leading to unauthorized access or data tampering. To remediate this vulnerability, update to version 2.2.0 or later, and ensure that user-provided data is properly sanitized or HTML encoded before deserialization. Additionally, consider implementing Content Security Policy (CSP) to restrict the execution of JavaScript in the application.

items.description: A vulnerability was determined in FoundationAgents MetaGPT up to 0.8.1. Executing a manipulation can lead to cross-site request forgery. The attack may be performed from remote. The exploit has been publicly disclosed and may be utilized. The project
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-6109 is a cross-site request forgery (CSRF) vulnerability in FoundationAgents MetaGPT up to version 0.8.1. The vulnerability is located in the evaluateCode function of the metagpt/environment/minecraft/mineflayer/index.js file within the Mineflayer HTTP API component. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability by executing a manipulation from a remote location, potentially leading to unintended actions being performed on the affected system. To remediate this issue, users should update to version 0.8.2 or later of FoundationAgents MetaGPT, as this version includes a fix for the CSRF vulnerability. Additionally, users should ensure that their systems are configured to prevent cross-site request forgery attacks by implementing appropriate security measures, such as validating user input and implementing CSRF tokens.

items.title: 22 BRIDGE:BREAK Flaws Expose 20,000 Lantronix and Silex Serial-to-IP Converters
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have identified 22 new vulnerabilities in popular models of serial-to-IP converters from Lantronix and Silex that could be exploited to hijack susceptible devices and tamper with data exchanged by them. The vulnerabilities have been collectively codenamed BRIDGE:BREAK by Forescout Research Vedere Labs, which identified nearly 20,000 Serial-to-Ethernet converters exposed

items.title: Google Fixes Critical RCE Flaw in AI-Based Antigravity Tool
items.description: The prompt injection vulnerability in the agentic AI product for filesystem operations was a sanitization issue that allowed for sandbox escape and arbitrary code execution.

items.title: ‘Scattered Spider’ Member ‘Tylerb’ Pleads Guilty
items.description: A 24-year-old British national and senior member of the cybercrime group "Scattered Spider" has pleaded guilty to wire fraud conspiracy and aggravated identity theft. Tyler Robert Buchanan admitted his role in a series of text-message phishing attacks in the summer of 2022 that allowed the group to hack into at least a dozen major technology companies and steal tens of millions of dollars worth of cryptocurrency from investors.

items.title: Third US Security Expert Admits Helping Ransomware Gang
items.description: Angelo Martino of Florida has pleaded guilty to collaborating with the BlackCat cybercrime group while working as a ransomware negotiator. The post Third US Security Expert Admits Helping Ransomware Gang appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Ransomware Negotiator Pleads Guilty to Aiding BlackCat Attacks in 2023
items.description: A third individual who was employed as a ransomware negotiator has pleaded guilty to conducting ransomware attacks against U.S. companies in 2023. Angelo Martino, 41, of Land O'Lakes, Florida, teamed up with the operators of the BlackCat ransomware starting in April 2023 to assist the e-crime gang in extracting higher amounts as ransoms. items.description: Masquerading as popular cryptocurrency wallets, the apps can hijack recovery phrases and private keys. The post Dozens of Malicious Crypto Apps Land in Apple App Store appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Stopping Fraud at Each Stage of the Customer Journey Without Adding Friction
items.description: Fraud prevention and user experience don't have to be a tradeoff. IPQS shows how combining identity, device, and network signals stops fraud without adding friction. [...]

items.title: UK probes Telegram, teen chat sites over CSAM sharing concerns
items.description: Ofcom, the United Kingdom's independent communications regulator, has launched an investigation into Telegram based on evidence suggesting it's being used to share child sexual abuse material (CSAM). [...]

items.title: EU targets two Russian propaganda networks with new sanctions
items.description: The measures target Euromore, a media outlet that EU officials say amplifies Kremlin narratives, and the Foundation for the Support and Protection of the Rights of Compatriots Living Abroad (Pravfond), a Moscow-funded group accused of promoting propaganda aligned with Russia’s foreign policy.

items.title: Cloud platform Vercel says company breached through third-party AI tool
items.description: Vercel released a statement acknowledging a breach and warning a “limited subset of customers” that their Vercel credentials were compromised.

items.title: 5 Places where Mature SOCs Keep MTTR Fast and Others Waste Time
items.description: Security teams often present MTTR as an internal KPI. Leadership sees it differently: every hour a threat dwells inside the environment is an hour of potential data exfiltration, service disruption, regulatory exposure, and brand damage.  The root cause of slow MTTR is almost never "not enough analysts." It is almost always the same structural problem: threat intelligence that exists

items.title: NGate Campaign Targets Brazil, Trojanizes HandyPay to Steal NFC Data and PINs
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have discovered a new iteration of an Android malware family called NGate that has been found to abuse a legitimate application called HandyPay instead of NFCGate. "The threat actors took the app, which is used to relay NFC data, and patched it with malicious code that appears to have been AI-generated," ESET security researcher Lukáš Štefanko said in a

items.title: Unsecured Perforce Servers Expose Sensitive Data From Major Orgs
items.description: Things are improving, but a researcher has still identified over 1,500 Perforce P4 instances allowing attackers to read files on the server. The post Unsecured Perforce Servers Expose Sensitive Data From Major Orgs appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: CISA flags new SD-WAN flaw as actively exploited in attacks
items.description: ​CISA has given U.S. government agencies four days to secure their systems against another Catalyst SD-WAN Manager vulnerability it flagged as actively exploited in attacks. [...]

items.title: Progress Patches Multiple Vulnerabilities in MOVEit WAF, LoadMaster
items.description: The security defects could be exploited for remote code execution, OS command injection, and WAF detection bypass. The post Progress Patches Multiple Vulnerabilities in MOVEit WAF, LoadMaster appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Chinese APT Targets Indian Banks, Korean Policy Circles
items.description: China is spying on India's financial sector, for some reason, and it's not putting much effort into it, judging by some stale TTPs.

items.title: No Exploit Needed: How Attackers Walk Through the Front Door via Identity-Based Attacks
items.description: The cybersecurity industry has spent the last several years chasing sophisticated threats like zero-days, supply chain compromises, and AI-generated exploits. However, the most reliable entry point for attackers still hasn't changed: stolen credentials. Identity-based attacks remain a dominant initial access vector in breaches today. Attackers obtain valid credentials through credential stuffing

items.title: Organizations Warned of Exploited Cisco, Kentico, Zimbra Vulnerabilities
items.description: CISA expanded the KEV catalog with eight flaws, but five of them have been flagged as exploited before. The post Organizations Warned of Exploited Cisco, Kentico, Zimbra Vulnerabilities appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Actively exploited Apache ActiveMQ flaw impacts 6,400 servers
items.description: Nonprofit security organization Shadowserver found that over 6,400 Apache ActiveMQ servers exposed online are vulnerable to ongoing attacks exploiting a high-severity code injection vulnerability. [...]

items.title: Data Breaches at Healthcare Organizations in Illinois and Texas Affect 600,000
items.description: Data breaches were disclosed by Southern Illinois Dermatology, Saint Anthony Hospital, and North Texas Behavioral Health Authority. The post Data Breaches at Healthcare Organizations in Illinois and Texas Affect 600,000 appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Google Patches Antigravity IDE Flaw Enabling Prompt Injection Code Execution
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have discovered a vulnerability in Google's agentic integrated development environment (IDE), Antigravity, that could be exploited to achieve code execution. The flaw, since patched, combines Antigravity's permitted file-creation capabilities with an insufficient input sanitization in Antigravity's native file-searching tool, find_by_name, to bypass the program's Strict

items.title: Former ransomware negotiator pleads guilty to BlackCat attacks
items.description: 41-year-old Angelo Martino, a former employee of cybersecurity incident response company DigitalMint, has pleaded guilty to targeting U.S. companies in BlackCat (ALPHV) ransomware attacks in 2023. [...]

items.title: $290 Million Kelp DAO Crypto Heist Blamed on North Korea
items.description: The hackers targeted LayerZero’s DVN, compromising certain RPCs and DDoSing others to trigger failover to the poisoned infrastructure. The post $290 Million Kelp DAO Crypto Heist Blamed on North Korea appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: NGate Android malware uses HandyPay NFC app to steal card data
items.description: A new variant of the NGate malware that steals NFC payment data is targeting Android users by hiding in a trojanized version of HandyPay, a legitimate mobile payments processing tool. [...]

items.title: A .WAV With A Payload, (Tue, Apr 21st)
items.description: There have been reports of threat actors using a .wav file as a vector for malware.

items.title: CISA Adds 8 Exploited Flaws to KEV, Sets April-May 2026 Federal Deadlines
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) on Monday added eight new vulnerabilities to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog, including three flaws impacting Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager, citing evidence of active exploitation. The list of vulnerabilities is as follows - CVE-2023-27351 (CVSS score: 8.2) - An improper authentication vulnerability in PaperCut

items.title: Ukraine busts ‘bot farm’ supplying thousands of fake Telegram accounts to Russian spies
items.description: Ukrainian authorities have dismantled a so-called “bot farm” that police say was supplying thousands of fake social media accounts to Russian intelligence services for use in disinformation campaigns against Ukraine.

items.title: KelpDAO suffers $290 million heist tied to Lazarus hackers
items.description: State-sponsored North Korean hackers are likely behind the $290 million crypto-heist that impacted the KelpDAO DeFi project on Saturday. [...]

items.title: China's Apple App Store infiltrated by crypto-stealing wallet apps
items.description: A set of 26 malicious apps on Apple App Store impersonate popular wallets, such as Metamask, Coinbase, Trust Wallet, and OneKey, to steal recovery or seed phrases and drain them of cryptocurrency assets. [...]

items.title: Vercel Employee's AI Tool Access Led to Data Breach
items.description: Stolen OAuth tokens, which are at the root of these breaches, "are the new attack surface, the new lateral movement," a researcher notes.

items.title: Serial-to-IP Devices Hide Thousands of Old & New Bugs
items.description: The OT devices that translate machine talk into Internet-speak are riddled with vulnerabilities and more frequently targeted for attacks, researchers say.

items.title: The Gentlemen ransomware now uses SystemBC for bot-powered attacks
items.description: A SystemBC proxy malware botnet of more than 1,570 hosts, believed to be corporate victims, has been discovered following an investigation into a Gentlemen ransomware attack carried out by a gang affiliate. [...]

items.title: Seiko USA website defaced as hacker claims customer data theft
items.description: The Seiko USA website was defaced over the weekend, displaying a message from attackers claiming they stole its Shopify customer database and threatening to leak it unless a ransom is paid. [...]

items.title: SGLang CVE-2026-5760 (CVSS 9.8) Enables RCE via Malicious GGUF Model Files
items.description: A critical security vulnerability has been disclosed in SGLang that, if successfully exploited, could result in remote code execution on susceptible systems. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-5760, carries a CVSS score of 9.8 out of 10.0. It has been described as a case of command injection leading to the execution of arbitrary code. SGLang is a high-performance, open-source serving

items.title: Italian regulator fines national postal service orgs $15 million for data privacy violations
items.description: The regulator fined Poste Italiane SpA, the postal service provider, €6.6 million ($7.8 million) and Postepay SpA, a digital payments subsidiary, €5.9 million ($7 million) for allegedly illegally processing millions of users’ personal data.

items.title: Crypto infrastructure company blames $290 million theft on North Korean hackers
items.description: A theft of nearly $300 million worth of cryptocurrency has been attributed to hackers from North Korea, as the industry grapples with the fallout of a wide-ranging incident involving multiple prominent platforms.

items.title: Serial-to-IP Converter Flaws Expose OT and Healthcare Systems to Hacking
items.description: Forescout researchers discovered 20 new vulnerabilities in Lantronix and Silex products and described theoretical attack scenarios. The post Serial-to-IP Converter Flaws Expose OT and Healthcare Systems to Hacking appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: WhatsApp Leaks User Metadata to Attackers
items.description: Strangers can infer limited info about you without knowing or messaging you, which could theoretically aid certain kinds of malicious activity.

items.title: ⚡ Weekly Recap: Vercel Hack, Push Fraud, QEMU Abused, New Android RATs Emerge & More
items.description: Monday’s recap shows the same pattern in different places. A third-party tool becomes a way in, then leads to internal access. A trusted download path is briefly swapped to deliver malware. Browser extensions act normally while pulling data and running code. Even update channels are used to push payloads. It’s not breaking systems—it’s bending trust. There’s also a shift in how attacks run.

items.title: British Scattered Spider Hacker Pleads Guilty in the US
items.description: Tyler Buchanan admitted in court to hacking into various companies, defrauding them, and stealing cryptocurrency from multiple individuals. The post British Scattered Spider Hacker Pleads Guilty in the US appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Hackers Abuse QEMU for Defense Evasion
items.description: The machine emulator has been abused in at least two different campaigns distributing ransomware and remote access tools. The post Hackers Abuse QEMU for Defense Evasion appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Why Most AI Deployments Stall After the Demo
items.description: The fastest way to fall in love with an AI tool is to watch the demo. Everything moves quickly. Prompts land cleanly. The system produces impressive outputs in seconds. It feels like the beginning of a new era for your team. But most AI initiatives don't fail because of bad technology. They stall because what worked in the demo doesn't survive contact with real operations. The gap between a

items.title: Handling the CVE Flood With EPSS, (Mon, Apr 20th)
items.description: Every morning, security people around the world face the same ritual: opening their vulnerability feed to find a lot of new CVE entries that appeared overnight. Over the past decade, this flood has become a defining challenge of modern defensive security. Some numbers[1]:

items.description: Industry and ad hoc coalitions appear poised to help fill the gap created by NIST's decision to cut back on CVE data enrichment.

items.title: Tycoon 2FA Phishers Scatter, Adopt Device Code Phishing
items.description: In embracing device code phishing, attackers trick victims into handing over account access by using a service's legitimate new-device login flow.

items.title: Every Old Vulnerability Is Now an AI Vulnerability
items.description: AI's danger isn't that it's creating new bugs, it's that it's amplifying old ones.

items.title: Coast Guard's New Cybersecurity Rules Offer Lessons for CISOs
items.description: The Maritime Transportation Security Act (MTSA) requires plans to protect OT systems, audits by independent third parties, and a hybrid OT-security role.

items.title: NIST Revamps CVE Framework to Focus on High-Impact Vulnerabilities
items.description: The National Institute of Standards and Technology is carving a new path for vulnerability remediation by changing the way it prioritizes software flaws.

items.description: &#x5b;This is a Guest Diary by Alec Jaffe, an ISC intern as part of the SANS.edu Bachelor&&#x23&#x3b;39&#x3b;s Degree in Applied Cybersecurity (BACS) program &#x5b;1].

items.title: Scanning for AI Models, (Tue, Apr 14th)
items.description: Starting March 10, 2026, my DShield sensor started getting probe for various AI models such as claude, openclaw, huggingface, etc. Reviewing the data already reported by other DShield sensors to ISC, the DShield database shows reporting of these probes started that day and has been active ever since.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, April 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft today pushed software updates to fix a staggering 167 security vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and related software, including a SharePoint Server zero-day and a publicly disclosed weakness in Windows Defender dubbed "BlueHammer." Separately, Google Chrome fixed its fourth zero-day of 2026, and an emergency update for Adobe Reader nixes an actively exploited flaw that can lead to remote code execution.

items.title: Russia Hacked Routers to Steal Microsoft Office Tokens
items.description: Hackers linked to Russia's military intelligence units are using known flaws in older Internet routers to mass harvest authentication tokens from Microsoft Office users, security experts warned today. The spying campaign allowed state-backed Russian hackers to quietly siphon authentication tokens from users on more than 18,000 networks without deploying any malicious software or code.

items.title: Germany Doxes “UNKN,” Head of RU Ransomware Gangs REvil, GandCrab
items.description: An elusive hacker who went by the handle "UNKN" and ran the early Russian ransomware groups GandCrab and REvil now has a name and a face. Authorities in Germany say 31-year-old Russian Daniil Maksimovich Shchukin headed both cybercrime gangs and helped carry out at least 130 acts of computer sabotage and extortion against victims across the country between 2019 and 2021.

items.title: ‘CanisterWorm’ Springs Wiper Attack Targeting Iran
items.description: A financially motivated data theft and extortion group is attempting to inject itself into the Iran war, unleashing a worm that spreads through poorly secured cloud services and wipes data on infected systems that use Iran's time zone or have Farsi set as the default language.

items.title: Feds Disrupt IoT Botnets Behind Huge DDoS Attacks
items.description: The U.S. Justice Department joined authorities in Canada and Germany in dismantling the online infrastructure behind four highly disruptive botnets that compromised more than three million hacked Internet of Things (IoT) devices, such as routers and web cameras. The feds say the four botnets -- named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid and Mossad -- are responsible for a series of recent record-smashing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks capable of knocking nearly any target offline.

items.title: Iran-Backed Hackers Claim Wiper Attack on Medtech Firm Stryker
items.description: A hacktivist group with links to Iran's intelligence agencies is claiming responsibility for a data-wiping attack against Stryker, a global medical technology company based in Michigan. News reports out of Ireland, Stryker's largest hub outside of the United States, said the company sent home more than 5,000 workers there today. Meanwhile, a voicemail message at Stryker's main U.S. headquarters says the company is currently experiencing a building emergency.

items.title: Microsoft Patch Tuesday, March 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft Corp. today pushed security updates to fix at least 77 vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and other software. There are no pressing "zero-day" flaws this month (compared to February's five zero-day treat), but as usual some patches may deserve more rapid attention from organizations using Windows. Here are a few highlights from this month's Patch Tuesday.

items.title: How AI Assistants are Moving the Security Goalposts
items.description: AI-based assistants or "agents" -- autonomous programs that have access to the user's computer, files, online services and can automate virtually any task -- are growing in popularity with developers and IT workers. But as so many eyebrow-raising headlines over the past few weeks have shown, these powerful and assertive new tools are rapidly shifting the security priorities for organizations, while blurring the lines between data and code, trusted co-worker and insider threat, ninja hacker and novice code jockey.

items.title: Who is the Kimwolf Botmaster “Dort”?
items.description: In early January 2026, KrebsOnSecurity revealed how a security researcher disclosed a vulnerability that was used to assemble Kimwolf, the world's largest and most disruptive botnet. Since then, the person in control of Kimwolf -- who goes by the handle "Dort" -- has coordinated a barrage of distributed denial-of-service (DDoS), doxing and email flooding attacks against the researcher and this author, and more recently caused a SWAT team to be sent to the researcher's home. This post examines what is knowable about Dort based on public information.

items.title: CISA Issues Updated RESURGE Malware Analysis Highlighting a Stealthy but Active Threat

Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
